quick_facts.label: Credential and purpose
quick_facts.value: B.Ed. (Bachelor of Education) is an NCTE-regulated professional teacher-education degree intended to prepare teachers for upper-primary/middle, secondary and senior-secondary school levels.

quick_facts.label: Usual regular duration
quick_facts.value: Under NCTE’s 2014 B.Ed. norms, the regular programme is two academic years and may be completed within a maximum of three years from admission.

quick_facts.label: Baseline entry qualification
quick_facts.value: The NCTE baseline is a bachelor’s and/or master’s degree with at least 50% marks; qualifying-mark relaxation and seat reservation follow the applicable Central, State or Union Territory rules. Institutions may publish more specific subject requirements.

quick_facts.label: Required practical component
quick_facts.value: The regular B.Ed. framework includes school internship and practicum, with minimum attendance requirements of 80% for coursework/practicum and 90% for school internship.

quick_facts.label: Teaching recruitment is separate
quick_facts.value: A B.Ed. alone is not an appointment guarantee. CTET eligibility and final appointment eligibility are determined under applicable NCTE minimum qualifications and the relevant recruiting authority’s rules.

quick_facts.label: Institution recognition matters
quick_facts.value: Applicants should verify that the particular teacher-education institution and B.Ed. programme are NCTE-recognised, in addition to checking university affiliation, the applicable admission bulletin and the current fee notice.

sections.heading: Who can pursue a B.Ed.?
paragraphs: For the regular two-year B.Ed., NCTE’s 2014 norms set a general minimum of 50% marks in a bachelor’s degree and/or master’s degree, or an equivalent qualification. Relaxation in marks and reservation are governed by the rules applicable to the relevant Central Government, State Government or Union Territory administration.

A university may impose programme-specific conditions on top of this baseline. For example, the University of Delhi’s 2025 B.Ed. bulletin required 50% for UR/OBC/EWS applicants and 45% for SC/ST/PwBD applicants in a bachelor’s or master’s degree; it also specified a higher engineering/technology threshold and required prior study of a teaching subject for its subject-category choices. This is an example of local admissions design, not a national rule for every B.Ed. provider.

Distance/ODL B.Ed. has separate conditions and is not simply an online substitute for the regular degree. IGNOU’s published B.Ed. programme information, for example, requires a recognised bachelor’s or higher degree and two years of full-time teaching experience in a recognised school for its in-service route.
bullets: A prior BA, BSc, BCom, BFA or other recognised bachelor’s degree can meet the general degree-level entry route, subject to marks, university-specific subject requirements and applicable reservation rules.
Applicants with a master’s degree may also qualify under the NCTE baseline.
Before applying, match your previous academic subjects with the school subject/pedagogy options offered by the college or university.

sections.heading: Duration and attendance
paragraphs: The established regular B.Ed. structure under NCTE’s 2014 norms is two academic years, with completion allowed within three years from the date of admission. The norms provide for at least 200 working days each year, a minimum 36-hour working week across five or six days, 80% attendance in coursework and practicum, and 90% attendance in school internship.

NCTE has published draft 2025 regulations that describe proposed one-year and two-year B.Ed. pathways. The one-year draft route is for applicants with a master’s degree or a specialised four-year undergraduate degree, while the two-year draft route is for applicants with a three- or four-year bachelor’s degree. Because this document is explicitly a draft released for public consultation, applicants should not assume that a one-year B.Ed. is available or applicable unless the regulator and the admitting institution formally notify it for their session.
bullets: Regular B.Ed.: normally two academic years under the NCTE 2014 framework.
Internship is a compulsory, attendance-sensitive part of the programme.
Confirm the duration, academic calendar and completion rules in the current prospectus of the specific institution.

sections.heading: What students study and do
paragraphs: NCTE’s B.Ed. framework is designed to combine subject knowledge, human development, pedagogical knowledge and communication skills. It groups learning into Perspectives in Education, Curriculum and Pedagogic Studies, and Engagement with the Field. The curriculum also integrates ICT, gender, yoga education, and disability/inclusive education.

In practice, students study education and learning, curriculum and assessment, and pedagogy for selected school subjects, while completing field tasks, school engagement and internship. The framework expects sustained work with the self, child, community and school rather than theory-only study. Exact papers, credits, languages of instruction and school-subject combinations vary by university.
bullets: Perspectives in Education: childhood, learning, society, curriculum and inclusion.
Curriculum and Pedagogy: disciplinary understanding, subject teaching methods, language/communication and assessment.
Engagement with the Field: assignments, practicum, professional-capacity work and school internship.

sections.heading: How admission commonly works
paragraphs: Admission procedures are set through the relevant State Government, university, affiliating body and institution, within NCTE norms. Applicants should expect an eligibility check, a prescribed selection route and document verification; the exact route may be a state-level entrance/counselling process, a university entrance test, or another procedure formally notified for that intake.

As a dated example rather than a universal rule, the University of Delhi’s 2025 bulletin required B.Ed. applicants to meet programme-specific eligibility and appear in the designated CUET paper. It also mapped applicants to subject categories and teaching subjects. Candidates should use only the current-year notice issued by their intended university or state authority, because exam routes, seats and dates can change.
bullets: Check NCTE recognition of the programme and the institution before paying fees.
Read the current admission bulletin for marks thresholds, subject prerequisites, reservation documents, entrance-test requirements and counselling steps.
Keep degree marksheets, identity/category certificates where applicable, migration/equivalence documents where applicable, and teaching-experience evidence for any in-service/ODL route.

sections.heading: Fees and cost checks
paragraphs: There is no single India-wide B.Ed. tuition figure. Under NCTE’s 2014 norms, an institution is to charge the fee prescribed by the affiliating body or State Government under the applicable fee-regulation framework, and it must not charge donations or capitation fees. Fees, refundable deposits, hostel expenses, examination charges and internship-related travel costs therefore vary substantially by state, university, college type and academic session.

Treat any published fee amount as session-specific. Obtain the official fee notice from the university or college and ask for a written break-up of tuition, university charges, examination fees, caution deposit, accommodation and any other compulsory charges before accepting admission.
bullets: Do not rely on aggregator fee estimates or verbal assurances.
Check whether the fee notice is for one year or the complete programme.
Avoid institutions seeking capitation fees or donations; retain receipts for every payment.

sections.heading: What a B.Ed. can lead to
paragraphs: A B.Ed. is a common professional qualification for school-teaching pathways, particularly for upper-primary through senior-secondary levels, but the required subject degree, TET/CTET qualification, recruitment rules and employer conditions vary by school system and post. CTET itself states that appearing for or passing the test does not create a right to appointment; eligibility is finally verified by the recruiting or appointing authority.

Graduates may also progress to M.Ed. The NCTE’s 2014 M.Ed. norms describe M.Ed. as a professional programme for teacher educators and education professionals such as curriculum developers, policy analysts, planners, administrators, supervisors, principals and researchers. Applicants must check the current M.Ed. eligibility rules of their chosen institution, as requirements and programme structures can change.

Beyond formal teaching recruitment, the degree’s pedagogy, curriculum and school-engagement training can support private-school teaching, tutoring, educational-content work and educational counselling. These roles are employer-defined; a B.Ed. does not by itself establish a statutory licence or guaranteed eligibility for every role.
bullets: School teaching: verify the post’s prescribed subject qualification, TET/CTET requirement and recruitment rules.
Further study: M.Ed. is the direct education-specialist progression; university-specific eligibility applies.
Education-sector roles: curriculum/content development, tutoring and counselling may value B.Ed. training, alongside subject expertise and employer-specific skills.
